1:
Applying Machine Learning to the Fuel Theft
Problem on Pipelines
Rachel Martins Ventriglia1; Leila Figueiredo Dantas, D.Sc.2; Bianca Brandão3;
Silvio Hamacher, D.Sc.4; Marcos Vinicius Bellé Rocha5; André Silveira David6;
and Frederico Chalita Ribeiro7
Abstract: Fuel theft of oil pipelines is a concern faced by various countries and oil and gas companies due to its impact on the envi-
ronment and the safety of neighboring communities. Oil pipeline monitoring and inspection with the support of alerts is essential to
preventing suspected fuel-theft events and mitigating risks. Monitoring systems trigger alerts, and patrols are sent to confirm the occur-
rence of illegal tapping. However, various signals can be activated briefly, and the correct prioritization is essential to identifying illegal
tapping as quickly as possible. This work aims to use machine-learning techniques to develop a predictive model capable of forecasting the
probability of an event resulting in illegal tapping and understanding the factors that influence the occurrence. A Brazilian oil and gas
transportation company provided data from a monitoring system supervised from January 2019 to August 2021. Five machine-learning
algorithms were used in this study: Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, Catboost, and Multilayer Perceptron. The Random
Forest obtained the best results in classifying alerts associated (or not) with an illegal tapping, showing accuracy, specificity, and sensi-
tivities of 76.8%, 68.3%, and 100%, respectively. In this problem, specificity implies reducing the sending of patrols to the field in 68.3%
of circumstances, and sensitivity means that the model is a good predictor for positive cases. As for the external validation, the model also
performed well, with an accuracy and specificity of 61%. The factors that most highly influenced illegal tapping occurrences were the alert
duration, previous events in the same area, and events during the night. DOI: 10.1061/JPSEA2.PSENG-1374. © 2023 American Society
of Civil Engineers.
Practical Applications: The problem of fuel theft of oil pipelines affects several countries worldwide and is a major concern due to its
impact on the environment and the safety of neighboring communities. Oil pipeline surveillance through monitoring systems is important
to prevent suspected fuel-theft events and mitigate risks. However, many alerts can be triggered in a brief period, and their correct
prioritization is essential to identifying illegal tapping as quickly as possible. This work used machine-learning techniques to develop
a predictive model capable of forecasting the probability of an event resulting in illegal tapping. Moreover, it has identified the factors
that most influence a fuel-theft occurrence: alert duration, previous events in the same area, and the night period. The developed model
can be used to assist companies in prioritizing alerts and improving the efficiency of patrol responses, contributing to combat, and
reducing fuel theft.
Author keywords: Machine learning; Fuel theft; Illegal tapping; Oil pipelines; Data science.
Introduction
Brazil is one of the largest oil producers globally, and the transport
of this fossil fuel and its derivatives is partially carried out through
oil and gas pipelines. According to ANP (2020), in 2019, the coun-
try had 530 pipelines used for the transportation of oil, derivatives,
natural gas, and ethanol, totaling 21,600 km of pipeline. Pipelines
represent the most efficient mode of transport and movement of oil
and fuel and are also safe if there is no third-party intervention.
However, one of the biggest problems carriers face is fuel theft
from pipelines. According to Petrobras (2020), 201 theft (or at-
tempted) cases were reported in 2020, preceded by 203 cases in
2019 and 261 in 2018. This unlawful practice, also known as illegal
tapping, presents a risk to the environment due to the possibility of
leaks, contamination of soil and rivers, fires, or explosions, and
may impact communities near the pipelines. In addition, the impact
of such crimes can reach more than US$40 million for Petrobras
annually (Veja 2019).
The fuel theft problem impacts Brazil and several countries
worldwide, including Mexico and Nigeria. According to GFI (2017),
a conservative estimate of crude oil theft’s worth is at least US
$5.2 billion to US$11.9 billion annually as of 2015. This estimate

2:
is based only on six countries: Colombia, Indonesia, Mexico, Syria,
Russia, and Nigeria, which all present high levels of crude oil
theft. According to Yale University (2020), this practice finances
several criminal organizations and is thus a primary global security
concern.
Monitoring and preventing the suspected fuel theft events in
pipelines is essential to mitigating risks and economic losses.
Usually, this monitoring is done by alarms, sensors, and systems
within the pipelines’ range, which indicate any depressurization,
leakage, or suspicious movement near it. When any suspicious
event is identified, or alarms are triggered near the pipelines, a team
is called to verify the location, confirm the event, and collect data
on specific areas. It is common for monitoring systems along the
pipelines to trigger many alerts in a short period. For this reason,
the company must identify the alerts that are more critical and more
likely to be confirmed as illegal tapping as quickly as possible.
Some studies focus on machine learning to detect and locate
leaks (Araujo et al. 2018; Oh et al. 2018a; Rashid et al. 2014;
Ribeiro et al. 2020). Instead, this study used these techniques to
identify which alerts are more likely to become a fuel theft event.
Therefore, this work aims to perform a predictive model capable of
(1) detecting the probability of an event resulting in an illegal tap-
ping; and (2) identifying the factors that influence the occurrence.
Principles of data science and machine learning techniques were
used, such as Logistic Regression and Random Forest. Applying
these techniques will help optimize the company’s handling of
these suspected fuel theft events, avoiding sending unnecessary pa-
trols to the site. Most importantly, the model aims to assist the
decision-making and the alerts’ correct prioritization.
The remainder of this work is organized as follows. The next
section provides a literature review of fuel theft literature and data
science techniques. Then, we present the methodology for data
analysis and model development, followed by the results and dis-
cussion. Finally, we summarize the findings, conclusions, and
limitations.
Literature Review
Due to the impact of the fuel theft problem, the constant monitoring
of oil pipelines is critical. Sensors inside the oil pipelines can iden-
tify oil leaks or suspicious events and send out alarms. Extracting
information from this data with data science techniques can be an
essential tool to rapidly identify the highest priority events.
Machine learning has been widely used to detect leaks or defects
in pipelines. Cruz et al. (2020) propose a technique that uses acous-
tic sensors with machine learning algorithms to identify and locate
leakages in low-pressure gas pipelines. Kim et al. (2021) used deep
neural network methods to train subsea gas pipelines’ dynamic leak
detection model. Rahmati et al. (2017) proposed a neural network–
based method for leakage detection of a gas pipeline by using a gas
flow pattern. Oh et al. (2018a) presented different machine learning
algorithms, such as Random Forest and XGBoost, to detect the
presence of leaks in pipelines.
The failure to detect events in pipelines carrying oil and gas was
approached by Rashid et al. (2014). They presented a distributed
detection system based on wireless sensor networks that identify
leakage in long-range pipelines. Machine learning methods are de-
scribed to improve these systems and evaluate the performance of
these sensors in detecting pipeline leaks, such as Support Vector
Machine (SVM) and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN).
Oh et al. (2018b) proposed a novel machine learning method to
efficiently diagnose high-pressure steam leakage in pipelines and
condense features from acoustic signals. Akinsete and Oshingbesan
(2019) used models to detect small leaks in a natural gas pipeline
using operational parameters such as pressure, temperature, and
flow rate. Such models performed better than a Real-Time Transient
Model, which, according to the authors, is the industry’s current pre-
ferred solution for leak detection in natural gas pipelines.
Kampelopoulos et al. (2020) compared the SVM and Decision
Trees (DT) models for leak detection in noisy industrial pipelines.
The data was gathered by a set of accelerometers on the surface of
the pipes to measure noise during normal operating conditions and
leaks. The most suitable model was the SVM. Araujo et al. (2018)
and Zaoui et al. (2020) addressed the application of machine learning
techniques to distributed temperature sensing (DTS), a technology
commonly used for continual monitoring of leaks in pipelines.
There are also some interesting data science applications in the
context of fuel theft. One example was proposed by Anifowose et al.
(2012), who explored the problem of oil pipelines interdiction and
the possible correlation between interdiction and essential socio-
economic variables. Obida et al. (2018) quantified the exposure of
humans and the environment to oil pollution in Nigeria using ad-
vanced geostatistical techniques. One of their aims was to identify
and analyze spill hotspots and the oil pipeline network. Results in-
dicated that pipeline sabotage accounted for 66% of all oil spilled
over nine years.
Fuel theft mainly occurs through the illegal tapping of oil pipe-
lines. Thieves can divert oil or other refined products from pipelines
by installing illegal taps. This practice can bring financial conse-
quences for carriers and detrimental damage to the pipelines. For
this matter, it is crucial to understand the factors that influence
the most in pipeline tapping. Hasan (2016) addresses this problem
and proposes a risk-based approach that uses an analytic hierarchy
process (AHP) model to determine the probability of sabotage in
different pipeline sections. Their findings show that pipelines lo-
cated around areas with a high crime rate, sparsely populated vicin-
ity, risk of landowner involvement, far-off police station, and leased/
government land presence are more likely to suffer from sabotage.
The problem of illegal tapping was also addressed by Exaudi
et al. (2018), who aimed to detect leakage of pipes by illegal tap-
ping and proposed pressure sensors for automatic detection. They
developed a leaking pipeline monitoring system using two pressure
sensors in real-time based on the pressure value changes. Ribeiro
et al. (2020) proposed a dynamic classifier selection technique on
multiple criteria, named overall local class-specific accuracy
(OLCA), to detect illegal tapping on pipelines. This system can
reduce the number of false positives and FNs when dynamically
selecting the classifiers of a baseline Random Forest ensemble.
Table 1 summarizes the works that used machine learning to
detect pipeline leaks (or fuel theft) and illegal tapping. To the best
of our knowledge, there are several studies on using machine learn-
ing to detect and locate leaks along oil pipelines. However, no stud-
ies applied these techniques to identify more likely alerts to become
a fuel theft event. Similar to Ribeiro et al. (2020), this work focuses
on the context of illegal tapping in pipelines. However, we propose
using machine learning techniques to perform a predictive model
capable of forecasting the probability of an alert that will result in
an illegal tapping. Moreover, like Hasan (2016), we also focus on
understanding the factors that influence the occurrence of this tap-
ping in a real oil and gas transportation company.
Methodology
This section describes the learning process step by step. The frame-
work used to develop the predictive models herein, adapted by
Dantas (2020), is presented in Fig. 1.

4:
and the “Number of events nearby.” The first one calculates the
number of illegal tappings that occurred up to one month before the
alert’s date. The second one calculates the number of events that
occurred within seven days before the date of the alert and within
five approximate channels. The channel is the identification num-
ber of the mileage where it was triggered. Both variables consider
that the illegal tapping or the event had to occur in the same alert
pipeline.
Two other features were considered variables for our problem:
“Duration,” which calculates the duration of the alerts in minutes,
and “Hour of the day,” which indicates the day of the sign that was
triggered, based on the hour it started. The “Hour of the day” var-
iable was created, grouping the hours into four time ranges: Dawn,
Morning, Afternoon, and Night. Each one represents a time span
of 6 h, beginning at midnight (0 h). These four features (“History
of illegal tapping,” “Number of events nearby”, “Duration,” and
“Hour of the day”) are the explanatory variables of our model.
In this problem, the dependent variable is the alert’s association
(or lack thereof) with an illegal tapping occurrence. The association
represents the positive class, whereas the nonassociation condition
means negative.
Visualization and Data Cleaning
A descriptive statistical analysis between the positive and negative
classes was performed. Continuous variables were compared using
Student’s t-test, and categorical variables were compared using the
chi-square test. The former tests the null hypothesis that the mean
of the positive and negative classes is statistically identical. The
latter tests the null hypothesis that the frequencies of each category
for the positive and negative classes are statistically the same. The
distribution of alerts and illegal tapping over time were also visu-
alized using a line plot.
Outlier detection and treatment did not identify any abnormal
values in this work. Also, there were no missing values in the var-
iables used.
Data Splitting
The data were divided into a training set, composed of 80% of the
data and a testing set, made up of the remaining 20%.
Data Preprocessing
The correlation between the variables was analyzed, dummy varia-
bles were created, and the variables were normalized (if necessary).
The preprocessing must be done separately for the training and test-
ing sets.
The Pearson method, the F statistic, and Omega-square were
employed to analyze the correlation between the variables. The for-
mer was used for correlation between numerical variables, while
the latter was used for categorical and numerical variables.
Normalization of numerical features to the range [0,1] was
necessary before following given procedures, such as XGBoost.
Some methods also required the conversion of categorical variables
into binary dummy variables. Each category will become one
binary variable, indicating whether the observation belongs to that
category.
Building Models—Training
The following classification methods were applied: Logistic
Regression; Random Forest; XGBoost; CatBoost; and Multilayer
Perceptron (MLP). Moreover, a tuned grid search was used to de-
termine the sets of hyperparameters that optimize each model fit.
Hyperparameters are model parameters that define their behavior
and must be outlined before we train the final model. The first
method used does not have hyperparameters, but the remaining
does.
Logistic regression is an example of a linear classification model
used when the data analysis describes the relationship between the
response variable and one or more explanatory variables. It was
selected due to its simplicity and straightforward interpretability
since making inferential statements about model terms is easy
(Kuhn and Johnson 2013). It takes the form of Eq. (1)
logitðpÞ ¼ log

¼ α þ βTx
ð1Þ
where α is the intercept parameter, x is a column vector of M in-
dependent variables, and βT contains the coefficients of the varia-
bles, which express the relationship between the variables and the
reference level (Hosmer and Lemeshow 2000). The likelihood of
an event or a specific class is represented by p, and the term
ðp=ð1 −pÞÞ is known as the Odds Ratio.
Tree-based classification methods segment the predictor space
into several simple regions according to a set of splitting rules that
can be summarized in a tree. Ensemble methods, such as Random
Forests and Boosting, use trees as building blocks to create more
powerful prediction models (James et al. 2013).
Random Forest combines random decision trees with Bagging
to achieve high classification accuracy, outperforming other learn-
ing methods (Sagi and Rokach 2018). This model has desirable
characteristics such as being robust to outliers and noise, faster than
bagging or boosting, and easily parallelized (Breiman 2001). The
Gini Index was used as the classification criteria to measure the
quality of a split, given by Eq. (2)
GINIðtÞ ¼ 1 −

ð2Þ
where pðjjtÞ is the relative frequency of class j in node t.
Boosting is an ensemble technique that tries to create a robust
classifier from weaker classifiers (Galar et al. 2012). It constructs a
model from the data and creates more models that attempt to correct
the prior model’s mistakes until it reaches a stopping criterion.
XGBoost, a scalable machine learning system for tree boosting,
was selected for presenting advantages such as its scalability and
capacity to run faster than existing solutions (Chen and Guestrin
2016). Catboost was selected for outperforming other gradient boost-
ing algorithms with decision trees as base predictors (Prokhorenkova
et al. 2018).
Lastly, a neural network model was selected due to advantages
such as high tolerance for noisy data and their ability to classify
patterns on which they have not been trained (Han et al. 2011).
The Multilayer Perceptron has one or more inputs, a bias, an acti-
vation function, and an output. The net is calculated by Eq. (3)
netj ¼

ð3Þ
where xi is the i-th input, wij is the weight of input i on neuron j,
and θj is the bias of neuron j.
A K-fold cross-validation technique was used to select the best
hyperparameters, avoiding overfitting and ensuring that the model
can be generalized. It divides the data set into k subsets. Splitting
the training data is repeated k times. The model is trained in all
subsets except one, (k-1), which is used to validate the model
according to a pre-defined metric. In the end, the average of the met-
rics is calculated for each k model. Five folds were chosen for the

5:
cross-validation technique to compute the best combination of hy-
perparameters. Once hyperparameters were selected, the models
were trained using the complete training set.
The Area Under the Curve (AUC) metric from the ROC curve
was adopted to evaluate each hyperparameter combination. The
ROC curve plots the True Positive Rate versus the False Positive
Rate at different classification thresholds. This method checks all
possible thresholds, and on an excellent classifier, the ROC curve
should be as near the top-left as possible. The hyperparameters
combination that resulted in the highest AUC was selected for each
model.
Selection of Optimal Cutoff
The measures used to evaluate a model’s performance are based on
a certain threshold applied to the output scores of the classifier. The
cutoff point is the probability that the model will categorize the
positive or negative classes. For example, a cutoff of 0.5 means
that the model will classify any observations with a probability
higher than 50% as positive (otherwise, negative). An issue with
this approach is that the built-in threshold applied to the scores
might not be suitable, especially for unbalanced data, causing low-
performance values concerning those criteria (Vanhoeyveld and
Martens 2018).
On the strength of discussions with the company and the aim of
the work, the main concern for the predictive model was to min-
imize the number of false negatives (FNs). These cases consist of
situations in which the model would classify an alert as not asso-
ciated with an illegal tapping when in fact, it was, which means
that the alert would not be correctly prioritized, leading to a po-
tential occurrence of fuel theft. For this reason, this was consid-
ered when determining the optimal cutoff threshold for the model.
The optimal cutoff value was selected based on the Youden Index
statistics provided by the ROC curve, giving greater weight to the
FNs. The goal is to minimize the FNs without jeopardizing the
other evaluation metrics. The optimal cutoff is the threshold
that maximizes the distance to the diagonal line given by Eq. (4)
(Youden 1950)
maxðw1 × sensitivity þ w2 × specificityÞ
ð4Þ
where the sensitivity is the True Positive Rate (TPR) and the
specificity equals 1−False Positive Rate (FPR). The weight is rep-
resented by w1 and w2.
We varied the values of the weights from 1 to 4 with a step of 0.5
and calculated the model’s evaluation metrics aiming to select the
weights that presented the best results, reducing the number of FNs
without greatly increasing the number of false positives, which was
our goal, as mentioned previously. Finally, the cutoff threshold was
calculated using the training set and based on the cross-validation
technique considering a weight three times higher for false negative
records.
Testing
Once the training was concluded, the final models were applied to
the testing set using the threshold chosen. The algorithms’ predic-
tions are compared to the testing data set with known outcomes to
establish the model’s performance.
Model Evaluation
Evaluation metrics based on the confusion matrix analysis were
used to evaluate the developed models. Metrics such as Accuracy,
Sensitivity, Specificity, Positive Predictive Value (PPV), Negative
Predictive Value (NPV), Precision, Recall, and F1-Score were ob-
tained. We focused on Accuracy and Specificity to evaluate the pre-
dictive model’s performance on the testing set results. The accuracy
is the default classification metric, but the specificity was also an
important metric to consider since it measures the models’ capacity
to predict the negative class. From an operational perspective, the
specificity shows how much the predictive model would reduce the
on-site patrols to verify an alert. Therefore, such criteria helped to
choose the final model for our prediction.
Running
The experiments were performed on an Intel® CORE i5 processor
with 8 GB of RAM and Python 3.8.5, using scikit-learn, pymatch,
pandas, matplotlib, and seaborn.
Results and Discussion
Data Preparation
We used the Event Inspection Database to identify the illegal tap-
ping events from the SIMF monitoring system and searched for
those events in the Alarm Monitoring Database. Considering
only “Digging” and “Mechanical Digging” alerts with high levels,
69 alerts associated with illegal tapping were identified, which were
considered the positive class. Alerts not associated were regarded
as a negative class, provided they adhered to the following prem-
ises: a patrol team verified the alert, and it was not a maintenance
alert. Moreover, we grouped into one same alert case the negative
alerts of the same pipeline within five channels and a time differ-
ence of 48 h.
The number of alerts not associated with an illegal tapping was
considerably higher than those associated with an illegal tapping,
causing an imbalance in the database. Building a predictive model
with this database would likely estimate all alerts negatively. There-
fore, a matched case-control study was performed to solve the
imbalance problem.
The matched case-control study allowed us to find a specific and
predefined number of negative alerts like each other according to
pre-established characteristics for each signal from the positive
class. In this study, we used a 3:1 proportion (controls: case), mean-
ing that we matched each alert from the positive class to three alerts
from the negative class. The variables used to match were the pipe-
line system (OPS) and the month. Three controls were selected for
each case occurring in the same OPS and month. We obtained a
database with 276 alerts through this technique, in which 69 are
from the positive class and 207 are from the negative class. This
database was used to build the predictive model. Fig. 2 presents all
filters applied in the database.
Visualization and Data Cleaning
To better understand the trends of alerts and illegal tapping occur-
rences, the data before the application of the matched case-control
study were plotted by month, as shown in Fig. 3. The blue line and
the left axis represent the 4,423 negative cases portrayed in the da-
tabase, that is, the alerts not associated with an illegal tapping. The
orange line and the right axis represent the 69 positive cases. It is
relevant to notice that the number of alerts is greater than the num-
ber of illegal tapping occurrences, showing that fuel theft is a
rarer event.
We performed a descriptive statistical analysis to better under-
stand and validate the data and variables, considering the data
after the matching, with 69 alerts linked to illegal tapping but 207

8:
The numerical variables and the “Night” category are highly sig-
nificant in our predictive model.
Despite not presenting the best performance, and consequently
not acting as the final predictive model, the Logistic Regression is
still particularly useful due to its straightforward interpretation.
Table 4 shows the coefficients and Odds Ratio (OR) for each var-
iable using multiple logistic regression. Through ORs, we can
understand the association of each variable with the response var-
iable of an alert being associated with an illegal tapping.
According to Table 4, the variables can be interpreted as fol-
lows: OR < 1 predicts a lower chance of the alert’s being associated
with an illegal tapping than its reference level, whereas OR > 1
indicates a higher probability. Thus, for each additional “History
of illegal tapping,” the alert has a 4.31 times higher chance of being
positive. On the other hand, the “Number of events nearby” vari-
able has the opposite effect: the chance is lower for alerts with
several events nearby. For the duration, each additional minute in-
creases the likelihood of an alert’s being associated with an illegal
tapping by 1.001. The “Night” category is the one most likely to
have alerts related to an illegal tapping (25 times higher than the
reference level “Dawn”). It is worth noticing that, despite a low
coefficient, the “duration” variable is an important variable to the
model (Fig. 4), and models trained without this variable did not
perform well.
A univariate analysis was also performed to understand if these
four variables can explain an illegal tapping alone. It concluded that
no single variable had a predictive capacity in the model.
External Validation
The external validation of the final predictive model used data col-
lected between June 2021 and August 2021. There were about
50,500 new alerts in the Alarm Monitoring Database, with four new
illegal tappings. The same steps of the data preparation and pre-
processing, including linking all the databases, were applied to the
new data.
After data preparation, the number of new registers from the
Alarm Monitoring Database dropped to 841 alerts (4 positive and
837 negative). Thus, the predictive model was validated using the
optimal cutoff of 0.2067 and the Random Forest model.
From the four registers of illegal tapping, three were correctly
classified by the model (“TP”), while one was labeled as negative
(“FN”). Out of the remaining alerts not associated with an illegal
tapping, 513 alerts were correctly classified as negative (“TN”),
whereas 324 were incorrectly classified as positive (“FP”). The
model’s accuracy was approximately 61%, as well as its specificity.
The latter means that the model can reduce by 61% the sending of
patrols on-site to inspect the alerts or their prioritization. The model
also presented a good sensitivity (75%), which is the ability to de-
tect positive cases.
The result of the accuracy of 61% was obtained due to the pri-
oritization of minimizing FNs. Thus, assigning a greater weight to
FNs impacted the increase of false positives in the result, gener-
ating a low accuracy. To compare the results, we used a cutoff of
0.5 for the classification of the alerts. In this case, out of the four
registers of illegal tapping, only one was correctly classified by
the model (“TP”), and the other three were labeled as negative
(“FN”). From the alerts not associated with an illegal tapping,
747 alerts were correctly classified as negative (“TN”), and only
90 were incorrectly classified as positive (“FP”). This model’s
accuracy and specificity were approximately 89%, but its sensi-
tivity was 25%.
Comparing the results of the external validation using the opti-
mal and the 0.5 cutoffs show us that the goal of minimizing the
number of FNs impacted the results. Although using a cutoff of
0.5 generated better accuracy and specificity, this model failed to
detect most of the positive classifications (3 “FN” and only 1 “TP”),
which is more critical in this problem since it means not associating
an alert with an illegal tapping when it was an illegal topping.
Using an optimal cutoff resulted in a lower accuracy, but a better
sensitivity (75%) since our model was able to classify most of the
positive cases correctly (1 “FN” and 3 “TP”). It provided better
results considering our main objective, reducing the number of
FNs. The prioritization decision will depend on the company and
the impact this can have on their business, the environment, and the
community.
Conclusions
A predictive model capable of forecasting the probability of an alert
resulting in an illegal tapping was developed considering four
variables: “duration”; “history of illegal tapping”; “number of events
nearby”; and “hour of the day.” The technique that provided the
best results was the Random Forest, similar to Ribeiro et al. (2020).
When applied to the test results, the model classified the alerts
into alerts associated (or not associated) with an illegal tapping with
an accuracy of 76.8%, specificity of 68.3%, and sensitivity of
100%, the last one meaning that the model can appropriately pre-
dict the positive cases. The specificity of this problem means that it
is possible to reduce the sending of patrols on-site in 68.3% of
cases. As for the external validation, the model also showed good
performance, with an accuracy and specificity of 61%, and sensi-
tivity of 75%.
According to our findings, the higher the alert duration, the
more likely it is associated with an illegal tapping. Alerts triggered
at night also have a higher probability of being associated with an
illegal tapping. Conversely, an increased number of events nearby
indicates a lower chance.
Transpetro uses the developed predictive model, contributing
to the reduction of on-site patrols, helping analysts to prioritize
alerts, and improving the efficiency of patrol responses. It also
enables further improvements to combat and reduce fuel theft.
From the social perspective, it promotes enhanced safety in
the communities near the pipeline and a reduction in environmen-
tal problems. Among the contributions to the literature, innova-
tions in the area stand out with the employment of machine
learning to predict the probability of an event resulting in an ille-
gal tapping.
The limitation of this study lies in the lack of integration be-
tween the databases used, which required us to perform them
manually. For future research, we propose the application of
balancing strategies to address the problem. We also suggest test-
ing other machine learning techniques, such as Support Vector
Machines.
